title: Around the Davenport-Heilbronn Function
speaker: Enrico Bombieri
abstract: The Davenport-Heilbronn function (introduced by Titchmarsh) is a linear combination of the two L-functions with a complex character mod 5, with a functional equation of L-function type but for which the analogue of the Riemann hypothesis fails. In this lecture, we study the Moebius inversion for functions of this type and show how its behavior is related to the distribution of zeros in the half-plane of absolute convergence. Work in collaboration with Amit Ghosh.

proof_stages.label: Functional equations are not by themselves decisive
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from Hamburger's uniqueness theorem for the zeta functional equation, then contrasts it with Epstein zeta functions and early off-critical-line zeros to pose the problem of what functional equations actually control.

proof_stages.label: Bohr almost-periodicity and zeros to the right
proof_stages.summary: Davenport-Heilbronn's method for translated zeta functions and related Epstein zeta functions uses almost-periodicity, multiplicative independence, character decompositions, and Cassels's algebraic input to force many zeros in the half-plane of absolute convergence.

proof_stages.label: Critical-line abundance for linear combinations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews Hardy-Littlewood, Selberg, Levinson, Voronin, Karatsuba, and Selberg-type methods showing that many, and sometimes a positive proportion of, zeros of suitable L-function combinations lie on the critical line.

proof_stages.label: Reciprocal Dirichlet series detect zeros through large coefficients
proof_stages.summary: Landau's theorem and its refinements show that if a Dirichlet series has zeros in its convergence half-plane, then the reciprocal Dirichlet series must have optimally large coefficients, including a weighted mean-square lower bound proved by a reverse density argument.

proof_stages.label: Davenport-Heilbronn functions as the minimal two-term test case
proof_stages.summary: The mod-5 complex Dirichlet L-functions are combined into the Davenport-Heilbronn functions τ± with a common functional equation, and numerical zero plots motivate questions about off-line zeros, density, and zero-free regions.

proof_stages.label: Euler-product and torus reduction of zero locations
proof_stages.summary: For a two-term combination, the zero equation is rewritten as a constant-value equation for a ratio of Euler products, and Bohr's theorem replaces vertical motion by independent motion on the prime torus.

proof_stages.label: Möbius inversion and generating functions on prime semigroups
proof_stages.summary: Restricting to semigroups generated by one or two primes, periodic local coefficients yield finite matrix recurrences and generating-function determinants whose radii of convergence give explicit coefficient-growth and zero-boundary exponents.

proof_stages.label: Verification and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The explicit formulas are checked numerically against zero and coefficient data, leading to open problems on Epstein zeta zeros, almost-all critical-line zeros from functional equations, density functions for longer combinations, and growing prime semigroups.

examples.name: Epstein zeta function for m^2+5n^2
examples.note: This class-number-two Epstein zeta function is presented as the early concrete counterexample showing that a functional equation alone does not enforce the Riemann hypothesis.
examples.engagement_reason: The Epstein zeta function for m^2+5n^2 is cited with reported numerical zero facts, but no computation or verification is carried out on it.

passes.reason: The Epstein zeta function of the ordinary positive definite form m^2+5n^2 is cited for an off-critical-line zero, showing the functional equation alone does not imply RH.
The discriminant -20/class-number-two Epstein zeta is the first minimal nonunique case and is used to exhibit a zero off the critical line despite the functional equation.

examples.name: Riemann zeta reciprocal under RH
examples.note: The cases f=ζ and then f=1/ζ, conditionally under RH, illustrate how Landau-type lower bounds for reciprocal Dirichlet coefficients can be sharp.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the reciprocal-series theorem under RH to f=1/zeta, identifies the convergence half-plane, beta, and coefficients, and checks sharpness of the lower bound.

passes.reason: Under RH the reciprocal-zeta example attains the stated lower bound sharply, so it functions structurally as a tight case rather than a counterexample.
The reciprocal of the basic zeta function is the trivial/base case whose coefficients make the lower-bound exponent sharp under RH.
the zeta reciprocal conditionally attains the coefficient lower bound sharply under RH
Under RH, f=1/zeta has reciprocal coefficients b_n=1, attaining the lower-bound exponent sharply rather than refuting it.
under RH the reciprocal example involving 1/zeta has coefficients attaining the stated lower-bound exponent sharply, so it is a tightness example
Under RH, 1/zeta(s) gives equality in the stated reciprocal-coefficient lower bound, so it is a sharp extremal example rather than a counterexample.

examples.name: Two-prime numerical test p=2, q=3
examples.note: The choice p=2 and q=3 is used as a numerical check comparing the direct Bohr-periodicity computation, the Möbius-inversion formula, and the observed coefficient/zero data.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the two-prime construction for p=2 and q=3, computes sigma and a maximizing parameter numerically, and compares the resulting formulas and plots.
